front: Work: this long poem opens with a 'pyramidal, funereal, death-born shadow of earth' aimed at heaven
back: First Dream / Primero sueno (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: this poem ends with 'the world illuminated, and I awake' as the golden sun dispenses colors to visible things
back: First Dream / Primero sueno (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: in this poem, the soul is liberated from the body by Night and travels through levels of knowledge; the soul is 'roused by her own precipitous fall'
back: First Dream / Primero sueno (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: this letter contains the quip 'if Aristotle had cooked, he would have written much more'
back: Reply to Sister Filotea (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: this letter was written in response to the Bishop of Puebla (Manuel Fernandez de Santa Cruz), who used the pseudonym 'Filotea'
back: Reply to Sister Filotea (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: this letter describes 'secrets of nature' learned from cooking and defends 'feminine' activities as pathways to knowledge
back: Reply to Sister Filotea (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: this letter thanks learned forebears like Aspasia and St. Gertrude, and quotes St. Jerome's letter To Leta
back: Reply to Sister Filotea (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: Octavio Paz praised this author's 'double solitude' as a woman and an intellectual in discussing this letter
back: Reply to Sister Filotea (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: this satirical redondilla mocks men, asking 'Why be outraged at the guilt that is of your own doing?' and accusing them of misting a mirror then complaining it's not bright
back: "You Foolish Men" / "Arraignment of the Men" (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: in this auto sacramental's loa, Religion spares two Aztecs named America and Occident; Zeal complains they exalt 'The God of Seeds'
back: Loa to Divine Narcissus / The Divine Narcissus (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)
work: Loa to Divine Narcissus / The Divine Narcissus

front: Work: this sonnet calls a portrait 'bright-colored deceit' and concludes ''Tis dead, 'tis dust, 'tis shadow, yea, 'tis nought'
back: "To Her Portrait" (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: this work describes this author's design for a triumphal arch and imagines Neptune as descending from Isis
back: Allegorical Neptune (Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz)

front: Work: this now-lost treatise was about the 'snail'-like spiral of justly-tuned fifths, inspired by Pietro Cerone and Athanasius Kircher
back: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz (lost music theory treatise)

front: Author: this author was known as the 'Tenth Muse' and 'American Phoenix'; child prodigy at the viceregal court of New Spain
back: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz

front: Author: this author was forced by Archbishop Aguiar y Seijas to sell her books and scientific instruments and cease writing
back: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz

front: Author: this author signed 'I, the worst of all women' in her own blood; a 1990 film about her is titled I, the Worst of All
back: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz

front: Author: this author refused to eat cheese as a child because she heard it made people stupid
back: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz

front: Author: this author wrote under the patronage of Tomas de la Cerda, the Viceroy of New Spain
back: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz

front: Author: Jose Medina's The Epistemology of Resistance calls this author an 'epistemic hero' for creating 'epistemic friction'
back: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz

front: Author: a posthumous portrait of this author by Miguel Cabrera shows her wearing a large oval Annunciation badge and sitting before a library shelf
back: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz
